rows.question: Is it a pencil?
rows.answer: Yes, it is. It's a pencil.

rows.question: Is it a crayon?
rows.answer: No, it isn't. It's a pen.

examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's a pencil.

examples.question: Is it a pen?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's a crayon.

examples.question: Is it a clock?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's a clock.

examples.question: Is it a map?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's a board.

examples.question: Is it a computer?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's a computer.

examples.question: Is it a table?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's a desk.

examples.question: Is it a crayon?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's a crayon.

examples.question: Is it a book?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's a notebook.

examples.question: Is it a classroom?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's a classroom.

examples.question: Is it a paper?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's a picture.

levelUp.title: Is it...? — Beyond the Classroom
levelUp.content: You already know how to ask 'Is it a pencil?' Great! But we use 'Is it...?' for so many more things in real life. We use it to ask about the weather, about time, about food, about animals, and even about feelings. Any time you want to ask YES or NO about ONE thing — use 'Is it...?'

examples.question: Is it cold today?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. Wear your jacket!

examples.question: Is it raining?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. You don't need an umbrella.

examples.question: Is it delicious?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. I love it!

examples.question: Is it a mammal?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It has fur.

examples.question: Is it late?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. We have time.

examples.question: Is it fun?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. I want to play again!

quiz.question: Which question is correct?
What does "it's" mean?
Which answer is correct for "Is it a pen?"
options: Yes, it is. It's a pen.
Yes, it are. It's a pen.
No, it isn't. It's a pen.
Yes, it is. It's a pencil.

quiz.question: What does "isn't" mean?
"Is it a clock?" Leo says "Yes, it is." Correct?
Is it cold today? Leo answers "Yes, it is." Which response is correct?
Which sentence uses "Is it...?" correctly?
"Is it dangerous?" — What does this question mean?
options: We are asking YES or NO about one dangerous thing

quiz.question: Fill in the blank: "___ it delicious?"
Which answer is correct for "Is it fun?"
"Is it a mammal?" — Leo answers "Yes, it is. It has fur." Is this correct?
Which is a real-world use of "Is it...?"

grammars.title: What and How many

notes: How many = asks about a number

grammars.explanation: We use WHAT to ask about a thing. We use WHAT COLOR to ask about color. We use HOW MANY to ask about a number. Remember: How many + plural noun.

levelUp.title: What and How many — In Real Life
levelUp.content: You use WHAT every single day! What is your name? What is your favorite food? What time is it? And HOW MANY helps you count everything around you — how many friends, how many books, how many goals did your favorite team score!

examples.question: How many players are on a soccer team?
How many goals did he score?
How many students are in your class?
How many days until the weekend?

quiz.question: Which question asks about a THING?
Which question asks about a NUMBER?
"What is it?" Leo answers "It's a pencil." Correct?
Which is correct?
"What color is it?" Leo says "It's blue." Correct?
Which answer is correct for "How many crayons?"
"What is your name?" — What word starts this question?
How many players are on a soccer team?
Which question is about color?
"How many days until the weekend?" Leo says "Two days." Is this a correct use of How many?
Fill in: "___ is it? It's a clock."
Which uses "What" correctly?

grammars.title: To be: is, are

notes: One thing = It's | Two or more = They're
It's = It is | They're = They are

grammars.explanation: We use IS for one thing. We use ARE for two or more things. It's is short for It is. They're is short for They are.

levelUp.title: Is and Are — Everywhere!
levelUp.content: IS and ARE are the two most important words in English. We use them every single day. IS is for one person, one thing, or one place. ARE is for you, we, they, or more than one thing. Once you master IS and ARE, English becomes much easier!

examples.question: It is a new video game.
They are my favorite soccer boots.
He is very excited about the match.
They are happy because their team won.

quiz.question: Which is correct for ONE thing?
Which is correct for TWO things?
"What is it?" Leo says "It's a tree." Correct?
What does "They're" mean?
Which is correct?
"What are they?" Leo says "They're mountains." Correct?
Choose the correct sentence:
Choose the correct sentence:
"It is a big stadium." Which word can replace "It is"?
"They are beautiful mountains." Which word can replace "They are"?
Which sentence is about TWO or more things?
"They are happy because their team won." Is this correct English?

grammars.title: To be: Where and in/on

notes: IN = inside something | ON = on top of / on a surface

grammars.explanation: We use WHERE to ask about a place. We answer with IN or ON. Use IN when something is inside: in the sky, in the box, in the classroom. Use ON when something is on a surface: on the wall, on the table, on the floor.

levelUp.title: Where? In and On — In Real Life
levelUp.content: WHERE, IN, and ON are words you use every single day. Where is my phone? It's on the table. Where is Leo? He's in the classroom. Where is the ball? It's in the goal! Once you know IN and ON, you can describe where anything is.

quiz.question: "Where is the clock?" What does WHERE ask about?
The bird is ___ the sky.
The book is ___ the desk.
"Where are the clouds?" Leo says "They're in the sky." Correct?
Which is correct?
options: Where is the pencil? It's on the table.
Where is the pencil? It's in the table.
Where is the pencil? They're on the table.
Where are the pencil? It's on the table.

quiz.question: "Where is the fish?" Leo says "It's in the river." Correct?
The food is ___ the kitchen.
The TV is ___ the wall.
"Where are the books?" — which answer is correct?
Where is Leo? He's ___ his room.
The soccer ball is ___ the floor.
Which uses IN correctly?
options: The clock is in the wall.
The birds are in the sky.
The book is in the desk.
The pencil is in the table.

rows.question: How many brothers do you have?
How many sisters do you have?

examples.question: How many brothers do you have?
How many sisters do you have?
I have one baby sister and two big brothers.
Do you have any siblings?
examples.answer: No, I don't have any siblings. I'm an only child.

examples.question: Do you have any pets at home?
examples.answer: No, I don't have any pets.

examples.question: Do you have any homework today?
examples.answer: No, I don't have any homework.

examples.question: Do you have any favorite books?
examples.answer: No, I don't have any favorite books yet.

examples.question: I have a photo of my family on the wall.
My family has a small dog as a pet.

levelUp.title: To Have - Possession & Relationships
levelUp.content: "Have" describes possession or relationships. Use "don't have any" for negatives with uncountable/plural nouns.

examples.question: She has long brown hair and big blue eyes.
He has a really funny sense of humor.
We have a soccer game every Saturday morning.
I have a great idea for our science project.
Do you have time to help me with this?
She doesn't have any money left after shopping.
He has a lot of friends at his new school.
They don't have any food in the refrigerator.
I have a feeling it's going to rain today.
Do you have what it takes to be a champion?

quiz.question: Which is correct?
"I don't have any sisters." means:
Fill in: "How many brothers ___ you have?"
Which is correct?
"Do you have any pets?" Leo says "No, I don't have any." Correct?
Fill in: "I ___ one baby sister."
Which uses "have" correctly?
"She has long brown hair." What does "has" tell us?
Which is the correct negative?
"Do you have time to help me?" This uses "have" to ask about:
Fill in: "He ___ a lot of friends."
Which sentence is correct?

grammars.title: He / She and questions with who

examples.question: Who's she?
examples.answer: She's my grandmother. She bakes delicious cookies.

examples.question: Who's he?
examples.answer: He's my big brother. He helps me with my homework.

levelUp.title: Who asks about people - He & She
levelUp.content: "Who" asks about people. In conversation, "Who's" is short for "Who is." The answer uses he/she + is.

examples.question: Who is that famous athlete?
examples.answer: He's LeBron James. He's the greatest.

examples.question: Who's that woman in the photo?
examples.answer: She's my aunt. She lives in Tokyo.

examples.question: Who's she?
examples.answer: She's my grandma. She's the best cook in the world.

examples.question: Who's your English teacher?
examples.answer: She's my dad. He's really strict!

examples.question: Who is the president of this club?
Who's she talking to?
examples.answer: She's talking to her best friend.

examples.question: Who's the new student?
examples.answer: He's Taro. He just moved here from Tokyo.

examples.question: Who's the fastest runner in your class?
Who's coming to your birthday party?
examples.answer: She's my cousin Anna. She's coming from Osaka.

quiz.question: "Who" asks about:
"Who's" is short for:
Fill in: "___ she? She's my sister."
"Who's he?" — He is a:
Which answer is correct for "Who's she?"
Fill in: "Who is that girl? ___ my best friend."
Which question is correct?
"Who's your favorite soccer player? He's Mbappe." Who is Mbappe?
Which is correct?
options: Who is that boy? He is my neighbor.
Who is that boy? She is my neighbor.
Who is that boy? It is my neighbor.
Who that boy? He my neighbor.

quiz.question: "She's my grandma." "She's" =
Fill in: "Who's that woman? ___ my aunt."
Which uses "who" correctly?

rows.question: Is there a table in the dining room?
Is there a sofa in the kitchen?

examples.question: Is there a table in the dining room?
Is there a sofa in the kitchen?
Is there a lamp in the bedroom?
Is there a TV in the kitchen?
Is there a mirror on the wall?
Is there a bed in the living room?
Is there a computer in the bedroom?
Is there a clock in the bathroom?
Is there a sofa in the living room?
Is there a microwave in the kitchen?

levelUp.title: Is there...? - Existence
levelUp.content: "Is there...?" asks if one thing exists somewhere. Answer: "Yes, there is" or "No, there isn't." We can also add information after the short answer.

examples.question: Is there a park near your house?
examples.answer: Yes, there is a park down the street.

examples.question: Is there a supermarket in this area?
examples.answer: Yes, there is one around the corner.

examples.question: Is there a good restaurant near here?
Is there a microwave in the kitchen?
Is there a problem with my homework?
Is there a swimming pool at your school?
examples.answer: No, there isn't one at our school.

examples.question: Is there a soccer game this weekend?
examples.answer: Yes, there is one on Saturday.

examples.question: Is there a movie theater in your town?
examples.answer: Yes, there is a big one near the station.

examples.question: Is there a shortcut to school?
examples.answer: No, there isn't one that I know of.

examples.question: Is there enough time to finish the test?

quiz.question: "Is there a table?" asks:
"isn't" =
Which short answer is correct?
Fill in: "Is there ___ lamp in the bedroom?"
"Is there a park near your house? ___, there is."
Which question is correct?
"Is there a swimming pool at school? No, there ___."

options: Is there a sofa in the kitchen?

examples.question: What's he doing?
examples.answer: He's sleeping in his cozy bed.

examples.question: What's Mom doing?
examples.answer: She's cooking a delicious dinner in the kitchen.

examples.question: What's Dad doing?
examples.answer: He's watching TV on the sofa.

examples.question: What's she doing?
examples.answer: She's cleaning her room with a broom.

examples.question: What's he doing?
examples.answer: He's taking a warm bath to get clean.

examples.question: What's she doing?
examples.answer: She's brushing her teeth before bedtime.

examples.question: What's he doing in the kitchen?
What's she doing with the paints?
What's he doing near the computer?

levelUp.title: Actions Now - He & She
levelUp.content: Present progressive (is + verb-ing) describes something happening RIGHT NOW. Use "What's he/she doing?" to ask. Add details to make sentences more interesting.

examples.question: What's he doing at the moment?
examples.answer: He's studying really hard for his test.

examples.question: What's she doing outside?
examples.answer: She's practicing her free kicks in the garden.

examples.question: What's he doing with his phone?
examples.answer: He's texting his friends about the match.

examples.question: What's she doing at the library?
examples.answer: She's reading the latest Harry Potter book.

examples.question: What's he doing on the computer?
What's she doing in the kitchen?
examples.answer: She's baking chocolate chip cookies for everyone.

examples.question: What's he doing right now?
examples.answer: He's training with the soccer team.

examples.question: What's she doing this afternoon?
examples.answer: She's helping her mom at the market.

examples.question: What's he doing with the guitar?
examples.answer: He's learning to play his favorite song.

examples.question: What's she doing at the park?
examples.answer: She's feeding the ducks with her little sister.

"He's sleeping." — "He's" =

Fill in: "What's she doing? She's ___ chocolate chip cookies."
Which is the correct present progressive?
"What's she doing at the park?" Best answer:

examples.question: I am wearing white shoes today.
He is wearing a suit and tie for the party.
She is wearing a swimsuit and a sun hat to the beach.
They are wearing warm coats, scarves, and gloves in winter.
I'm wearing a blue shirt and jeans today.
She's wearing a purple jacket and brown shoes.
He's wearing socks and sneakers for PE class.

levelUp.title: Wearing Clothes Now
levelUp.content: Present progressive with "wearing" describes what someone has on RIGHT NOW. We use am/is/are depending on the subject.

examples.question: What are you wearing to the school festival?
examples.answer: I'm wearing my traditional Japanese clothes.

examples.question: What is Leo wearing for the soccer match?
examples.answer: He's wearing his team jersey and shorts.

examples.question: What is your mom wearing today?
examples.answer: She's wearing a beautiful blue dress.

examples.question: Why are you wearing a raincoat?
He's wearing headphones because he's listening to music.
They're wearing matching t-shirts for the team photo.
What is the goalkeeper wearing?
examples.answer: He's wearing gloves to protect his hands.

examples.question: Why is he wearing a cast?

quiz.question: "I'm wearing" =
"What ___ you wearing?"
Fill in: "She ___ wearing a purple jacket."
Which is correct?
"What are they wearing?" Answer:
Fill in: "I'm wearing my lucky ___."
"What is Leo wearing for the match?" Best answer:
Why use "is wearing" not "wears"?
Fill in: "They're wearing matching ___."
"What is the goalkeeper wearing?" He says "He's wearing gloves." Correct?
Which is correct?
options: Why you are wearing a raincoat?
Why are you wearing a raincoat?

quiz.question: Fill in: "I ___ wearing my traditional clothes."

notes: Use "that" for one thing far away, "those" for many things far away.

examples.question: What are those in the closet?
What's that?
examples.answer: That's a skirt. It's my sister's.

examples.question: What are those on the table?

levelUp.title: That & Those - Distance
levelUp.content: "That" points to ONE thing at a distance. "Those" points to MANY things at a distance. Compare: "this/these" (near) vs "that/those" (far).

examples.question: What are those people doing?
examples.answer: Those are my teammates. They're warming up.

examples.question: What's that noise outside?
examples.answer: That's a car alarm going off.

examples.question: What are those lights in the sky?
examples.answer: Those are fireworks! It's a festival.

examples.question: What's that smell?
examples.answer: That's my mom's cooking. She's making curry.

examples.question: What are those numbers on the board?
What's that animal in the tree?
What are those?
examples.answer: Those are my soccer trophies. I won them last year.

examples.question: What's that on your arm?
examples.answer: That's a bruise from yesterday's match.

examples.question: What are those uniforms?
examples.answer: Those are the away kits for our soccer team.

quiz.question: "That" is used for:
"Those" is used for:
"What's that?" — "What's" =
Fill in: "What are ___? Those are my shoes."
Which is correct?
Fill in: "___ that? That's a monkey!"
"What are those lights in the sky?" Best answer:
"What's that building?" — how many buildings?
Fill in: "What's ___? That's my mom's cooking."
Which is correct for many things?
"What are those? Those are my soccer trophies." Correct?
Fill in: "___ are those uniforms? Those are the away kits."

rows.question: Does he want a truck?
rows.answer: No, he doesn't. He wants a train.

examples.question: Does he want a truck?
examples.answer: No, he doesn't. He wants a train.

examples.question: Do you want a bike?
examples.answer: Yes, I do. I love bikes!

examples.question: Does she want a game?
examples.answer: No, she doesn't. She wants a doll.

examples.question: Do you want a car?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I want a robot.

examples.question: Does she want a top?
examples.answer: No, she doesn't. She wants a kite.

examples.question: Do you want a teddy bear?

levelUp.title: Simple Present - Wanting Things
levelUp.content: Use "want" with I/you/we/they. Use "wants" with he/she/it. For questions: Do + I/you/we/they or Does + he/she/it.

examples.question: Do you want to play soccer after school?
examples.answer: Yes, I do! I always want to play soccer.

examples.question: Does he want to be a professional soccer player?
examples.answer: Yes, he does. It's his dream.

examples.question: Does she want to learn Japanese?
examples.answer: No, she doesn't. She already speaks it!

examples.question: Does Leo want to study more English?
examples.answer: Yes, he does. He wants to get better.

examples.question: Do they want to go to the movies tonight?
examples.answer: Yes, they do. There's a great new film.

examples.question: Do you want some chips?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I want some fruit instead.

examples.question: Does your team want to win the championship?
examples.answer: Yes, they do! They train every day.

examples.question: Do you want me to help you?
Does she want a new phone?
examples.answer: Yes, she does, but it's too expensive.

examples.question: Do you want to know a secret?

quiz.question: "Do you want?" uses:
"Does she want?" uses:
"don't" =
"doesn't" =
Fill in: "___ she want a ball?"
Fill in: "Do you want a bike? Yes, I ___."
"He wants a train, not a truck." "wants" is used because:
Which is correct?
options: Does he want a drum? Yes, he do.
Does he want a drum? Yes, he does.

quiz.question: Fill in: "Does she want a game? No, she ___."
"Do you want to play soccer? Yes, ___ do."
Which is correct?
options: Do Leo want to study English?
Does Leo want to study English?

quiz.question: Fill in: "___ your team want to win? Yes, they do!"

rows.question: Is this your teddy bear?
rows.answer: No, it isn't. It's Sonia's teddy bear.

rows.question: Are these your puppets?
rows.answer: No, they aren't. They're Mark's puppets.

examples.question: Is this your teddy bear?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's Sonia's teddy bear.

examples.question: Are these your puppets?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. They're Mark's puppets.

examples.question: Is this your puzzle?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's my puzzle.

examples.question: Are these your dolls?
examples.answer: Yes, they are. They're my dolls.

examples.question: Is this your robot?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's my robot.

examples.question: Are these your board games?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. They're Anita's board games.

examples.question: Is this your bike?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. It's my brother's bike.

examples.question: Are these your kites?
examples.answer: Yes, they are. They're our kites.

examples.question: Is this your ball?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. It's my soccer ball.

examples.question: Are these your cars?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. They belong to my cousin.

levelUp.title: This & These - Close By
levelUp.content: Use "this" for ONE nearby thing. Use "these" for MANY nearby things. "Is this...?" for singular, "Are these...?" for plural.

examples.question: Is this your homework?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. I just finished it.

examples.question: Are these your test results?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. Mine are better!

examples.question: Is this your seat on the bus?
examples.answer: Yes, it is. I saved it.

examples.question: Are these your sports shoes?
examples.answer: Yes, they are. I got them for my birthday.

examples.question: Is this the right classroom?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. Room 3B is upstairs.

examples.question: Are these the latest FC24 player cards?
examples.answer: Yes, they are! Look at the ratings!

examples.question: Is this your phone?
examples.answer: No, it isn't. Mine has a blue case.

examples.question: Are these your books on the desk?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. I left mine at home.

examples.question: Is this a good time to talk?
Are these the ingredients for the recipe?
examples.answer: Yes, they are. Let's start cooking!

quiz.question: "This" is for:
"These" is for:
"Is this your robot?" — How many robots?
"Are these your dolls?" — How many dolls?
Fill in: "___ this your puzzle? Yes, it is."
Fill in: "___ these your board games? No, they aren't."
"isn't" =
"aren't" =
Which is correct?
"Is this your seat? Yes, ___ is."
"Are these the FC24 cards? Yes, ___ are!"
Fill in: "___ these your sports shoes? Yes, they are."

examples.question: Our feet are tired after the soccer game.

levelUp.title: My, Your, His & Her
levelUp.content: Possessive adjectives (my/your/his/her) show WHO something belongs to. They come BEFORE the noun. Never use apostrophe with possessive adjectives.

examples.question: My favorite soccer team is Real Madrid.
My dream is to play in the World Cup one day.
His free kick technique is amazing.
examples.answer: I want to learn from him.

examples.question: My bedroom is covered with soccer posters and trophies.
Our school's soccer team is the best in the region.

quiz.question: "My" shows:
Fill in: "___ hair is black." (talking about I)
Fill in: "___ arm is strong." (talking about a boy)
Fill in: "___ eyes are brown." (talking about a girl)
Fill in: "___ feet are tired." (talking about you)
Possessive adjectives come:
options: At the end of the sentence.

quiz.question: "My favorite team is Real Madrid." "My" shows:
options: Which team belongs to me/I support.

quiz.question: Which is correct?
Fill in: "___ bedroom is covered with soccer posters." (I)
Fill in: "___ English is getting better every day." (You)
"His free kick technique is amazing." "His" shows the technique belongs to:
Fill in: "___ name is Leo." (He)

examples.question: Can you jump high?
examples.answer: Yes, I can. I have strong legs!

examples.question: I can draw pictures. Can you draw too?
She can jump very high. She's a great athlete.
He can run faster than anyone in our class.
Can I swim in the pool?
examples.answer: Yes, you can. You took swimming lessons.

examples.question: Can she ride a bicycle?
examples.answer: Yes, she can ride without any help.

examples.question: Can he speak Japanese?
examples.answer: Yes, he can. His parents are from Japan.

examples.question: Can they play the piano?
examples.answer: No, they can't. But they can play guitar.

levelUp.title: Ability with Can
levelUp.content: "Can" shows ability — something you ARE ABLE to do. "Can't" shows inability. Can is the same for all subjects (I/you/he/she/we/they can).

examples.question: Leo can dribble a soccer ball better than anyone his age.
Can you do a bicycle kick?
examples.answer: Not yet, but I'm practicing every day!

examples.question: She can speak three languages: Japanese, English, and Spanish.
Can artificial intelligence write stories?
examples.answer: Yes, it can, and it's getting very good.

examples.question: Can a cheetah run faster than a lion?
examples.answer: Yes, it can. It's the fastest land animal.

examples.question: Can you do a backflip?
examples.answer: No, I can't — but I can do a cartwheel!

quiz.question: "Can" shows:
options: What you are able to do.

quiz.question: "can't" =
"Can" is:
Fill in: "Can you jump high? Yes, I ___."
Fill in: "Can he run fast? No, he ___."
Which is correct?
"Leo can dribble better than anyone." Can shows:
Fill in: "Can a cheetah run faster than a lion? Yes, ___ can."
Which is correct?
options: I can not play guitar now.
I cannot play the guitar not.

quiz.question: Fill in: "Can she speak Japanese? Yes, ___ can."
"Can you do a bicycle kick? Not yet." "Not yet" means:
Fill in: "We ___ solve this if we work together."

grammars.title: Like with count and noncount nouns

rows.question: Do you like apples?
rows.answer: No, I don't. I don't like apples.

rows.question: Do you like bananas?
rows.answer: Yes, I do. I like bananas.

rows.question: Does he like soup?
rows.answer: Yes, he does. He likes soup.

rows.question: Does she like fish?
rows.answer: No, she doesn't. She doesn't like fish.

notes: Noncount nouns have no plural (soup, fish, rice).

examples.question: Do you like bananas?
examples.answer: Yes, I do. I like bananas.

examples.question: Do you like apples?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I don't like apples.

examples.question: Does she like rice?
examples.answer: Yes, she does. She likes rice.

examples.question: Does he like soup?
examples.answer: No, he doesn't. He doesn't like soup.

examples.question: Do you like cheese pizza?
examples.answer: Yes, I do. I love it!

examples.question: Does she like cookies?
examples.answer: Yes, she does. She likes cookies very much.

examples.question: Do you like fish?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I don't like fish at all.

examples.question: Does he like chicken?
examples.answer: Yes, he does. He likes chicken for dinner.

examples.question: Does she like milk?
examples.answer: No, she doesn't. She doesn't like milk.

levelUp.title: Count & Noncount Food
levelUp.content: Use "like" with I/you/we/they. Use "likes" with he/she/it. Count nouns = things you can count (apples, cookies). Noncount nouns = things you can't count (soup, rice, milk). Don't add -s to noncount nouns.

examples.question: Does Leo like playing FC24?
examples.answer: Yes, he does. He loves it!

examples.question: Do you like action movies?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I prefer comedy.

examples.question: Does she like studying English?
examples.answer: Yes, she does. It's her favorite subject.

examples.question: Do they like Japanese food?
examples.answer: Yes, they do. Especially ramen and sushi.

examples.question: Does your cat like fish?
examples.answer: Yes, it does. It goes crazy for fish!

examples.question: Do you like rainy days?
examples.answer: No, I don't. I prefer sunny weather.

examples.question: Does he like spicy food?
examples.answer: No, he doesn't. It's too hot for him.

examples.question: Do you like Minecraft?
examples.answer: Yes, I do. I can play it for hours.

examples.question: Does she like mornings?
examples.answer: No, she doesn't. She's not a morning person!

examples.question: Do they like our new teacher?
examples.answer: Yes, they do. He's very funny.

quiz.question: "Like" is used with:
"Likes" is used with:
Which is a COUNT noun?
Which is a NONCOUNT noun?
Fill in: "Does she like ___?"
Fill in: "Do you like cookies? Yes, I ___."
Fill in: "Does he like soup? Yes, he ___."
Which is correct?
"Does Leo like playing FC24?" Best answer:
Fill in: "Do they like Japanese food? Yes, ___ do."
Which is correct for noncount nouns?
Fill in: "Does she like ___? No, she doesn't. She's not a morning person."

notes: Don't use a/an with noncount nouns: cheese, rice, soup, water, milk, juice.

examples.question: He ordered a sandwich for lunch.
Can I have an orange, please?
She baked a cookie for me.
I want an egg for breakfast.
He's drinking a glass of milk.
She has an egg and a banana for breakfast.
Can I have an orange juice, please?

levelUp.title: A or An?
levelUp.content: "A" before consonant sounds (b, c, d, f, g...). "An" before vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u). The rule is about the SOUND, not just the letter. "An hour" (h is silent, sounds like "our"). "A university" (sounds like "you").

examples.question: I want to be an engineer when I grow up.
He's reading an interesting book about space.
She has an umbrella because it might rain.
He ate an entire pizza by himself!
It takes an hour to walk to the stadium from here.

quiz.question: "A" is used before:
"An" is used before:
Fill in: "I want ___ banana."
Fill in: "She's eating ___ apple."
Which word does NOT use "a" or "an"?
Fill in: "I need ___ eraser for the test."
Fill in: "It takes ___ hour to walk there."
Fill in: "She is ___ excellent student."
Which is correct?
options: He ate the entire pizza a.

quiz.question: Which does NOT need a/an?
Fill in: "Can I have ___ orange, please?"
Fill in: "He's ___ unique player."

examples.question: Are the cows eating?
examples.answer: Yes, they are. They're eating grass.

levelUp.title: They are... -ing (Present Progressive)
levelUp.content: "They are + verb-ing" describes what a group is doing RIGHT NOW. Use "they're" for short. Ask "What are they doing?" or "Are they...?" for yes/no.

examples.question: What are the players doing?
examples.answer: They're warming up before the big match.

examples.question: Are the fans cheering?
examples.answer: Yes, they are! The stadium is incredible.

examples.question: What are the students doing?
examples.answer: They're studying for their final exams.

examples.question: What are they building?
examples.answer: They're building a new sports facility.

examples.question: Are your friends coming to the party?
examples.answer: Yes, they are! All of them.

examples.question: What are the scientists doing?
examples.answer: They're researching a cure for the disease.

examples.question: Are they watching the game?
examples.answer: Yes, they're watching it live on TV.

examples.question: What are the birds doing?
examples.answer: They're migrating south for the winter.

examples.question: Are your parents home?
examples.answer: No, they aren't. They're still at work.

quiz.question: "They're" =
"aren't" =
Fill in: "What are the horses doing? They're ___."
Fill in: "Are the sheep sleeping? No, they ___."
Which is correct?
"Are the ducks swimming?" Answer:
Fill in: "What are they doing? They're ___ for the match."
"Are the fans cheering?" They say "Yes, they are!" Correct?
Fill in: "Are your parents home? No, they ___. They're still at work."
"They're building a new sports facility." This means:
Which question is correct?
Fill in: "Are the birds migrating? Yes, they ___ migrating south."

rows.question: Do you want to ride the donkey?
What do you want to do?
rows.answer: I want to ride the horse.

rows.question: What does Anna want to do?
rows.answer: She wants to see the ducks.

examples.question: Do you want to ride the donkey?
What do you want to do?
examples.answer: I want to ride the horse.

examples.question: What does Anna want to do?
examples.answer: She wants to see the ducks.

examples.question: Does Ivan want to climb?
examples.answer: No, he doesn't. He wants to ride the horse.

examples.question: I want to see the cows.
She wants to swim with the ducks.
Do you want to fly like a bird?
He wants to watch the frogs jump.
They want to learn how to swim like the ducks.
She wants to go to the farm and see all the animals.

levelUp.title: Want to (Infinitive)
levelUp.content: "Want to + base verb" expresses a wish or desire. I/you/we/they + want to. He/she/it + wants to. The base verb never changes — no -s, no -ing.

examples.question: I want to become a professional soccer player someday.
He wants to learn how to code and build his own app.
Do you want to play FC24 after school?
She wants to read all of the Harry Potter books this summer.
Does he want to go to university in England?
examples.answer: Yes, he wants to study there.

examples.question: We want to make Leo's Academy the best learning platform in the world!

quiz.question: "Want to" is followed by:
"She wants to see the ducks." "wants to" is used because:
Fill in: "I ___ to ride the horse."
Fill in: "She ___ to see the ducks."
Which is correct?
"Do you want to fly like a bird?" Answer:
Fill in: "Does Leo want to improve his English? Yes, he ___ to become fluent."
Which is correct?
Fill in: "I want to eat sushi. What do ___ want?"
"We want to make Leo's Academy the best!" "want to" is used because the subject is:
Which is correct?
Fill in: "___ he want to go to university in England?"
